name: Afl Cio Employees
description_short: Federal credit union headquartered in Washington, D.C., serving AFL–CIO members and families since 1952 with $53.6M in assets.
description_long:
AFL–CIO Employees Federal Credit Union was established in 1952 as a member-owned financial cooperative specifically designed to serve the labor movement. Originally chartered as a federally chartered credit union, it has grown to serve approximately 9,723 members across two locations in the Washington, D.C. area. The organization operates as a multiple common bond credit union, meaning eligibility extends beyond just AFL–CIO employees to include members of affiliated international unions, constitutional departments, state and local central labor bodies, and select partner organizations.

The credit union offers a comprehensive suite of consumer financial services including savings and checking accounts, consumer loans, mortgage products, credit cards, and online banking. As of its most recent annual report (fiscal year ending December 31, 2015), the institution held $53.6 million in total assets. The organization provides traditional banking services tailored to the needs of union members and their families, with a focus on affordable lending and financial security.

What distinguishes AFL–CIO Employees FCU from commercial banks is its mission-driven structure as a labor-affiliated credit union and its eligibility requirements tied to union membership. The organization specifically serves Office and Professional Employees International Union Local 2, United Food and Commercial Workers Local 400, the National Football League Players Association, and International Union of Painters and Allied Trades District Council 51, among others. This specialized focus allows the credit union to understand and address the unique financial needs of working families and union households.

The credit union experienced significant challenges during the 2008 economic downturn, suffering large losses in the first half of that year—a common experience for many financial institutions during the financial crisis. Members should note that eligibility is restricted to specific union affiliations and labor organizations rather than being open to the general public, and the institution operates only two physical locations in the Washington, D.C. region.
services: Member financial services for union households
pros: Federally chartered credit union with mission alignment to labor movement and union members
Offers comprehensive financial products including savings, checking, consumer loans, mortgages, and credit cards
Serves 9,723 members with $53.6M in assets as of 2015
Provides online banking access for convenience
Specialized focus on union members and their families through tailored products
Eligibility extended to multiple union affiliates including OPEIU, UFCW, NFLPA, and IUPAT members
cons: Restricted membership eligibility—only open to specific AFL–CIO affiliated organizations and partner unions, not general public
Limited physical presence with only two branch locations in Washington, D.C. area
Experienced significant losses during 2008 financial crisis
Relatively small institution compared to national banks with $53.6M in assets
Limited asset growth and recovery information provided beyond 2015 annual report
best_for: AFL–CIO employees and members of affiliated international unions seeking union-mission-aligned banking
Union members in Washington, D.C. area needing comprehensive financial services with labor movement values
OPEIU Local 2, UFCW Local 400, NFLPA members, and IUPAT District Council 51 members requiring eligible credit union membership
diagnosis: AFL–CIO Employees FCU is best for union members and labor organization employees in the D.C. area seeking banking services aligned with labor movement values. The primary limitation is strict membership eligibility tied to specific AFL–CIO affiliations, making it inaccessible to the general public.
typical_results_timeline: Membership approval and account opening typically takes 1-3 business days. Loan decisions are usually faster than traditional banks.
